response: The managerial problem is to set an integer hourly staffing roster before a service window begins, balancing wage expense against the expected cost of customer waiting while satisfying shift-coverage rules. The supplied operations log establishes that this is a recurring advance decision and records the relevant burden in the observed windows: arrival counts, service times, wages, and waiting times. In the model, scheduled staff determine service capacity; stochastic arrivals and service times then produce queue lengths and waits. This makes the decision significant because too little scheduled capacity can increase expected waiting cost, whereas more capacity raises labor cost. The study can compare modeled roster alternatives on the stated expected-cost objective. It cannot claim that the optimized roster has already caused lower realized waiting or labor cost without implementation or causal evidence. Nor does the supplied log support generalization beyond its recorded service windows. Any realized-savings or broader-effect claim therefore remains unsupported.
